summary: This interview features an engineering manager discussing how to prioritize features when resources are limited, using data-driven approaches and established escalation processes. The conversation then shifts to a scenario involving a critical bug in a feature (a progress bar) that threatens a deadline. The manager outlines strategies for assessing impact, considering deadline extensions, making trade-offs when shipping with known bugs, communicating delays to stakeholders, and maintaining team morale. The interview concludes with general advice for aspiring engineering managers.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Let's say your engineering team has to build two features, but there's only bandwidth to build one of them. What do you do as the engineering manager?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Well, part of it depends on the company, so I'll tell you about a couple of different companies I've worked at. For example, when I worked at Walmart, it was a very data-driven company. There, I led an engineering team that worked on a mobile website, and I would get requests all the time for features. In this case, say we had two features, and we only had bandwidth to build one. We had a North Star, which was to increase the conversion rate of the website. That was the most important thing; it came from senior management, plus everyone on the team agreed that this would be the most impactful thing. So when product managers would come with feature requests, I would always ask them, 'How much do you think this is going to move the conversion rate?' 'Do you have a theory for how much it'll improve it or how it will improve it?' If we had any kind of data to show, that would be even better. Then the team would do, so that's the data they would give us, and then the team could estimate the effort. So the engineering team would estimate the effort of the feature, and we would look at the impact-to-effort ratio: 'How much is it going to move the conversion rate based on the effort?' In this case, if we had two features, if they were equal effort, we'd look at which one would improve the conversion rate more, or that we believed would improve it more. Otherwise, we would just do this impact-effort calculation in that case.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Cool, so it sounds like you would look at the company goal, and then you would also look at some matrix consisting of impact and effort.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, 'How does this feature move the big goal we have?' In this case, that team was to improve the conversion rate, so that was very clear. At another company I worked on, we had a list of prioritized programs. So if two different features came in, we would see which program they mapped to, and the higher priority program would get preference, unless there was some extenuating circumstance. I'll just say two more things there which I think are helpful: One, it helps that the product managers who are coming to you, whoever's asking for the features, kind of understands that process. They understand, 'Hey, this is how we're thinking about it. We're trying to move this metric, or we're looking at this list of prioritized programs, and this is what we're going to do,' so that they buy into it. But we also give them an escape valve. We say, 'Okay, if you don't like the result, no offense, it's fine. Here's how you can escalate. You can escalate to these people, and if they agree with you, maybe we're wrong, and then we'll do the other feature.' So as long as there's a clear escalation path and the process is clear, it's fine. This happens all the time. I mean, this is like an engineering manager's job a lot when you're doing planning, whether you do quarterly planning, yearly planning, any kind of planning.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: What does that escalation process look like? Do you give them like the engineering directors, or who do you link them up with?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Well, so at LinkedIn, we have a very nice process for this. We have this rapid process which you may have read about. So there are discussions between, say, me and the other engineering manager. Then you can involve their managers, try to find a common point where there's a decision-maker. Maybe we both fall into the same VP, for example, so then the VP might be a decision-maker there. There's usually some negotiation. We'll write up a document which lists the pros and cons of all the approaches and what the recommendations are. So it'll be spelled out a little bit. I don't want to say it's like a legal brief, but the arguments will be laid out in writing, and then that will be sent up the chain, and presentations will be made, and a decision will be handed down.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: I like that. I think a writing culture really helps people get their thoughts out and helps people be more comprehensive in thinking about decisions. So that sounds good for choosing one of the two that you have to build. Let's imagine that the one that you choose is a progress bar, for example, in the UI. And let's imagine that there are some edge cases in the bugs. So the team is building this progress bar, and sometimes the progress bar goes past 100%, sometimes it goes to a negative percentage when it's not supposed to, and the team is not going to meet the deadline. What would you do here?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Well, these things happen also. So I would look at, first of all, how important is it to meet the deadline? Can we extend the deadline? What would it take to fix this bug, and is it acceptable to extend the deadline to deliver the bug? It could be, I don't know what product this is going into, but say, I mean, it's not a good customer experience. So I'm always thinking about the customer a lot because I manage front-end engineering teams, I've managed mobile engineering teams where things, I like stuff that customers can touch, and it doesn't look good if the progress bar goes. So that would be one thing. How much, what's it going to take to fix it? It's some amount of time. You usually can't get an extension, but I'd want to ask anyway, 'Is that a possibility?' 'What's the impact of this being late?' Maybe it's no big deal if it's late an extra day or two, but maybe it's a really big deal. Maybe this is holding up some other, it's not usually just the progress bar that's going out, right? It's going out, I would assume it's part of a larger project. So now if that's going to hold up a larger deliverable which is going to have impacts, then we probably can't hold it up just for the progress bar. So then we're going to have to look at, 'What is the impact if the progress bar goes negative or positive?' It looks bad, but is any user data going to be lost? That would be the first thing. If user data is going to be lost, or some financial impact, or regulatory violation, there's some really bad thing like that, then we might have to say, 'Wait, we really have to stop this because you can't lose user data, we can't lose financial information, legally protected stuff.' But a progress bar doesn't seem likely that it would have those kinds of serious negative impacts.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, so that makes sense for in the event that the progress bar cannot be moved. So let's maybe talk about what could you do as the engineering manager if you learned that you can move the deadline?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Well, if we can move the deadline, I mean, we might fix the bug. I think you're just using it as an example, right? Like a progress bar is like a very tiny thing. But if we could move the deadline, there's no real impact to moving it. I mean, there's also the cause, here's the other thing too, maybe we wouldn't even move the deadline. It's not that big a deal. I guess I really understand what is the impact again. I really hate to send stuff out that doesn't look good, but software does ship with bugs. Every piece of software out there has known bugs that it goes out with. So maybe if the impact's not that bad, and there's lots of other goodness, because the other is if we delay it, then all the other goodness which is going out this feature will also be delayed, which might have far more negative impact to delay. So you could just put it in the release notes, sure.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: What does it say to your team though if you're allowing them to ship software with these known bugs?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Well, look, I want to keep a high bar for everything, but the reality is software does ship with bugs. Video games ship with bugs, all kinds of things have, they're not perfect. Sometimes we don't know what the bugs are. This is a known bug. I don't like to, I'd like it to be at a high level of craftsmanship, but I mean, these are things that I'd be kind of trading off in my mind. How many people are going to see this? So you say the progress bar might go negative or over 100%. How many people, how likely is it that a customer is going to see this? If it's like one in a million customers in an odd edge case, then it's not that important. If you're saying every customer is going to see this bad experience, then I'm much more concerned. So I guess I want to understand how many people are going to see this. If it's a very, very tiny number, maybe that's acceptable because to delay it would, like say one percent of people are going to have this experience, but if I delay it, 100% of people aren't going to have the other experience which is better by rolling the thing out. You'd have to make that kind of trade-off, I feel.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, are there any sort of frameworks that you use at work when it comes to moving deadlines, or is it mainly just thinking about the impact and going from there?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: It's a good question. I don't know if there's a formal framework that I'm aware of. I'm sure there is, there are formal frameworks for almost everything. I can't think of one off the top of my head. I guess I would look at the impact and try to see how many customers are going to be impacted. What's going to be the impact? Is it just cosmetic? Is it a devastating data loss thing? And you have to make a judgment call whether it's worth delaying the product launch for this defect versus rolling it out and fixing it later, knowing, as you say, it's not perfect. Nobody likes to send stuff that's not perfect, but you can't be a perfectionist either.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Got it. You mentioned something interesting where you mentioned how this could impact the release of a broader product. And I wanted to ask you, let's say that the delay of this progress bar impacts the downstream roadmap for a larger, maybe some super group, product. How would you communicate that impact to the roadmap to your stakeholders?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Well, then you'd have to communicate it. So then it's not just a question of me deciding to delay the progress bar. Now it's like, 'I have to move this release out because I have an issue that we believe we need to fix.' And so now we're not going to be able to start work on the next phase, which is going to impact downstream roadmaps. I'd have to talk with those other teams, let them know that I'm thinking of delaying the release because I have this issue. And maybe they're okay with it, they're like, 'That's fine,' or maybe they're not okay with it, like, 'No, you can't do that.' So I'd have to discuss. I just wouldn't do it unilaterally. I'd have to talk with them and say, 'So what's going on? What do you think?' assuming I think it's really like, 'We really have to delay the release because this progress bar thing is so bad.'

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Got it. Yeah, so let's say you've done all this. The stakeholders, the people you work with, the leaders in the team are all happy with the direction, even though there is this delay. Now, how would you, what would you say to the team, I guess, just to keep team morale high and make them feel confident in their work?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, I would say that we're going to fix the issue. We want to have a good customer experience. I wouldn't try to blame anyone for it. Maybe at our retrospective, or if we had to do a post-mortem of this, you could do that, try to understand how this happened so that we could prevent it happening in the future. I mean, no one, again, no one intentionally would code a progress bar like that. So I don't assume any bad intent on anyone's part. It's just like, 'Oh, interesting, progress bar. How did that happen? What can we learn in the future? Let's fix it, let's move on.' But these would be some of the thoughts I'd have.

training_pairs.answer: Well, then you'd have to communicate it. So then it's not just a question of me deciding to delay the progress bar. training_pairs.question: How would you say to the team just to keep team morale high and make them feel confident in their work?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, I would say that we're going to fix the issue. We want to have a good customer experience. I wouldn't try to blame anyone for it. Maybe at our retrospective, or if we had to do a post-mortem of this, you could do that, try to understand how this happened so that we could prevent it happening in the future. How did that happen? What can we learn in the future? Let's fix it, let's move on.' But these would be some of the thoughts I'd have.
